title: Data Visualization in Unity: Charts, Graphs, Heatmaps
summary: Overview of building in-scene data visualizations in Unity — bar charts, scatter plots, network graphs, heatmaps, and state/flow diagrams. Unity is increasingly used for dashboards, analytics overlays, and digital twins where external data is rendered in world space (3D) or on Canvas (2D UI). Use whenever a Mosaic tool emits a visualization from external data: chart/create-bar, chart/create-scatter, data/bind, data/stream, process/state-diagram, process/flow-diagram, and related routing via procgen/tool-selection. Covers geometry choices (UI Image vs cube mesh vs instanced quad), color mapping (viridis, RdBu, tab10), layout algorithms (force-directed, hierarchical), and the performance cliff where > ~1,000 points requires instanced rendering instead of one-GameObject-per-datum. The #1 failure mode is spawning a GameObject per data point at scale; the #2 is rebuilding geometry on every data update instead of updating buffers or MaterialPropertyBlocks.

llmGuidance.when_asked_to_visualize_tabular_data: Pick geometry by count and space. ≤ 100 points and on-screen UI → Canvas + UI Image (bars) or RectTransform dots. > 100 in world space → cube/quad mesh per datum for small sets, instanced rendering for > 1,000. Build once, update values in place.
llmGuidance.when_asked_to_build_a_scatter_plot_in_3d: Use Graphics.RenderMeshIndirect with a StructuredBuffer of points (see kb-rendering-compute-buffer-lifecycle). One draw call regardless of count. Encode scalar/category in instance color via a second buffer or MaterialPropertyBlock channel.
llmGuidance.when_asked_to_draw_a_network_or_graph: Nodes = instanced quads or sphere primitives; edges = LineRenderer per edge for ≤ 500 edges, one Mesh with dynamic vertex stream for > 500. Default layout = force-directed (Fruchterman-Reingold). Use hierarchical (Sugiyama) when data is a DAG with a clear root.
llmGuidance.when_asked_for_a_heatmap: 2D heatmap → generate a Texture2D with SetPixels32 or LoadRawTextureData, render on a RawImage or quad. 3D heightfield heatmap → a plane Mesh whose vertex.y is driven by data and vertex color by a sampled palette. Do not build one cube per cell for grids > 50x50.
llmGuidance.when_asked_to_animate_data_changes: Lerp the underlying data buffer or per-instance value, not the GameObject transforms. Bars → tween scaleY over ~200–400 ms with smoothstep. Streaming series → FIFO shift in a ring buffer, pulse the newest bar/point via emissive color.
llmGuidance.when_asked_for_a_flow_or_state_diagram: Lay out boxes by layer (Sugiyama) or dot-style rank/cluster, then route arrows as cubic Bezier curves with arrowhead meshes at the end. Do not depend on Graphviz at runtime — bake layouts offline or implement a minimal layered layout in C#.
llmGuidance.when_choosing_a_color_palette: Continuous scalar → viridis or magma (perceptually uniform, colorblind-safe). Diverging around zero → RdBu or BrBG. Categorical ≤ 10 classes → tab10 or Okabe-Ito. Never use raw HSV rainbow — it is perceptually non-uniform and misleads magnitude.
llmGuidance.when_user_reports_frame_drops_as_data_up: Inspect the update path. If it rebuilds meshes, destroys and re-spawns GameObjects, or re-creates Materials per frame, that is the cost. Switch to updating a MaterialPropertyBlock or SetData on a persistent GraphicsBuffer. Geometry topology rarely changes; values do.
llmGuidance.when_user_reports_data_looks_garbled_or_: Confirm the index mapping between data row and instance slot is stable. Sorting the underlying list between frames causes instance N to receive a different data row — animate by value change, not by re-ordering indices.
llmGuidance.when_user_wants_tooltips_or_click_intera: Cast a ray from the mouse (Canvas → GraphicsRaycaster, world → Camera.ScreenPointToRay). For instanced draws, meshes have no per-instance colliders — maintain a CPU-side spatial index (KD-tree or uniform grid) and hit-test against data points directly.

geometryChoices.canvasUI: RectTransform + Image for 2D bar charts, legends, axis labels. Cheap up to a few hundred elements. Layout via HorizontalLayoutGroup or anchored math. For axis text use TextMeshPro (SDF scales cleanly at any zoom). See kb-ui-ui-systems-overview.
geometryChoices.worldSpaceMesh: One Mesh with per-instance cube primitives baked into a combined mesh — good for 100–1,000 bars that are static in topology but dynamic in height. Update via a single Mesh.SetVertices call or per-instance scale via a MaterialPropertyBlock matrix.
geometryChoices.instancedRendering: For > 1,000 points use Graphics.RenderMeshIndirect (Unity 2022+) or Graphics.DrawMeshInstancedIndirect (legacy). A StructuredBuffer<Point> holds position + value + category. Material samples it via unity_InstanceID. Lifecycle rules: see kb-rendering-compute-buffer-lifecycle.
geometryChoices.lineRenderer: Built-in LineRenderer is fine for < ~100 discrete edges or polyline series. Above that, author a dynamic Mesh with a custom shader — LineRenderer allocates per-segment geometry each enable and chokes on thousands of lines.
geometryChoices.texture2D: Heatmaps and raster imagery. For a W x H grid, allocate Texture2D(W, H, TextureFormat.RGBA32, false), call SetPixels32(array) + Apply(false). Render on a Quad (world) or RawImage (Canvas). Keep mipmaps off for pixel-accurate display.

chartTypes.barChart: Vertical or horizontal bars. Scale on one axis encodes magnitude. In 3D, use a Cube primitive with localScale.y = value * verticalScale and position.y = localScale.y / 2. In 2D, use an Image with preferredHeight or a scaled RectTransform. Group bars by adjusting x spacing; stack bars by summing cumulative y offsets.
chartTypes.scatterPlot: Each datum is a point in 2D or 3D. In 3D, points are typically billboarded quads or low-poly spheres to preserve on-screen size under perspective. Encode extra dimensions via color (via palette), size (instance scale), or opacity. For > 10k points, GPU-side culling via compute shader is worth the complexity.
chartTypes.networkGraph: Nodes as instanced spheres/quads, edges as line segments. Two layout families: force-directed (Fruchterman-Reingold, spring-electrical) for organic blobs with unknown structure, hierarchical (Sugiyama layered layout) for DAGs with ranks. Iterate layout on-CPU for < 1k nodes or on a compute shader for larger graphs.
chartTypes.heatmap2D: Colored grid. Palette lookup happens either CPU-side (write RGBA into Texture2D per cell) or GPU-side (single-channel R32F texture + a LUT texture the shader samples). GPU path is faster for streaming data because SetPixels32 marshals the full array every update.
chartTypes.heatmap3DTerrain: Data drives vertex height on a regular grid mesh (resolution = W x H). Color via vertex color (Mesh.colors) or a shader sampling the same data texture. Rebuild normals (Mesh.RecalculateNormals) when heights change, or compute them in a compute shader.
chartTypes.stateFlowDiagram: Boxes (state nodes) + arrows (transitions). Layout boxes on a Sugiyama layered grid: rank by longest path from source, compact within rank, minimize edge crossings by barycenter heuristic. Route arrows as cubic Bezier curves from box edge to box edge with arrowhead meshes.

layoutAlgorithms.forceDirected: Fruchterman-Reingold: each node is a charged particle repelling others by inverse-square force, each edge is a spring pulling endpoints together. Integrate with damping for 50–500 iterations. O(N^2) naive; O(N log N) with a Barnes-Hut quadtree. Suitable for < 10k nodes on CPU; ports to compute shader for larger sets.
layoutAlgorithms.hierarchicalSugiyama: Four phases: (1) break cycles via DFS, (2) assign ranks by longest path, (3) order within ranks to minimize edge crossings (barycenter or median heuristic), (4) assign x/y coordinates. Produces readable DAG layouts (state machines, call graphs, pipelines).
layoutAlgorithms.tree: Reingold-Tilford for binary or n-ary trees. Recursively lay out subtrees, then translate so they don't overlap. Runs in O(N) and produces tidy, readable trees — the right choice for hierarchical data with no cross-edges.
layoutAlgorithms.gridLayout: Trivial but often best for small categorical sets. Place nodes in a rows x cols grid. No iteration needed. Use when the user just wants everything visible at once without occlusion.

colorMapping.continuousSequential: Viridis (blue→green→yellow), magma (black→red→yellow), inferno (black→purple→yellow). All perceptually uniform, colorblind-safe, print-safe. Sample as a 256-entry LUT texture and index by (value - min) / (max - min).
colorMapping.continuousDiverging: RdBu (red→white→blue), BrBG (brown→white→green), PiYG (pink→white→green). Use when data has a natural zero or midpoint (profit/loss, anomaly from mean). Center the white band on the midpoint, not on the arithmetic middle of the range.
colorMapping.categorical: tab10 (10 distinct hues), tab20 (20 hues), Okabe-Ito (8 colorblind-safe). Pick by category count. Above ~12 categories, consider pattern/shape encoding instead — humans cannot distinguish 20 hues reliably.
colorMapping.avoidRainbow: Hue-based rainbow palettes (red→orange→yellow→green→blue) are NOT perceptually uniform — yellow bands appear brighter than adjacent hues, implying magnitude spikes that aren't there. Also fails for red-green colorblind users. Use viridis instead.
colorMapping.perInstanceColorBinding: Bind via MaterialPropertyBlock.SetVectorArray("_Colors", colors) for < 1023 instances per draw, or a StructuredBuffer<float4> for unbounded counts sampled via unity_InstanceID. Update only when category or value changes — not every frame.

animation.valueTween: For bar/scatter updates, interpolate the stored data value over ~200–400 ms with smoothstep easing, not the transform directly. The render loop reads the eased value and updates scale or buffer contents. Keeps easing consistent across geometry paths.
animation.streamingSeries: For time-series that grow over time use a ring buffer of fixed size. New data overwrites oldest slot; render reads slot (head - i) % N to lay out left-to-right. No allocation, no GC churn.
animation.highlightPulse: For 'new data point arrived' cue, pulse the point's emissive color channel from bright to normal over 500 ms. Implement as a per-instance float 'newness' in the StructuredBuffer, decremented per frame by Time.deltaTime / 0.5f, clamped ≥ 0, sampled by the shader.
animation.layoutTransitions: When switching layouts (e.g. grid → force-directed), tween each node's position from old to new over 1–2 s. Store both positions in the buffer and lerp in the shader via a global _LayoutT uniform — avoids a per-frame C# loop.

interaction.pickingUI: Canvas hit-testing goes through GraphicsRaycaster automatically. Attach an IPointerClickHandler on the bar's GameObject. Free — no raycast code needed.
interaction.pickingWorld: World-space raycast: Ray r = Camera.main.ScreenPointToRay(Input.mousePosition); Physics.Raycast(r, out hit). Requires a Collider per datum — does NOT work for instanced draws. For instanced scatter/networks, maintain a CPU-side KD-tree keyed on world position and hit-test against data points.
interaction.tooltip: On hover/click, spawn a small Canvas panel positioned at screen-space mouse coord with text from the underlying data row. Use a pool of 1–4 tooltip panels; never Instantiate/Destroy per hover.
interaction.zoomPan: For 3D views, a camera-orbit rig (yaw, pitch, distance) driven by mouse drag + scroll. For 2D UI charts, scale the content RectTransform and clamp anchored position to the viewport. Both decouple input from the data layer.
interaction.filter: Apply filters by masking the instance count (args[1]) after sorting matching data to the front of the buffer, or by passing a filter mask texture the shader samples to discard non-matching instances. The second path avoids re-ordering data.

performance.oneGameObjectPerDatumAntiPattern: The #1 mistake. At 10k points, Instantiate/Destroy and per-frame Transform updates saturate the main thread. Symptoms: linear frame-time scaling with data size, GC spikes on data refresh. Switch to instanced rendering — one draw call, one buffer, no GameObjects.
performance.updateInPlace: Geometry topology (vertex/index data) should be allocated once. Per-frame updates go through MaterialPropertyBlock.SetFloatArray, GraphicsBuffer.SetData on a persistent buffer, or Texture2D.SetPixels32 on a persistent texture. Rebuild only when the schema itself changes (e.g., a new category).
performance.materialPropertyBlock: MaterialPropertyBlock overrides per-renderer (or per-instance) material values without breaking SRP batching. For < 1023 instances per draw it exposes SetVectorArray/SetFloatArray/SetMatrix4x4Array. Beyond that, move to StructuredBuffer + instanced indirect.
performance.asyncReadback: When charts display live GPU simulation output, NEVER call buffer.GetData() on the main thread. Use AsyncGPUReadback.Request with a callback 1–3 frames later. See kb-rendering-compute-buffer-lifecycle.
performance.textAllocations: TextMeshPro text updates allocate when the string changes. For live labels, use SetText(StringBuilder) or SetCharArray — both skip the managed-string allocation. Updating 100 labels/frame with = "..." adds up to real GC pressure.

decisionRules.scenario: Visualization has ≤ 100 data points and lives inside a Canvas UI
decisionRules.rule: Build from UI Image + RectTransform per datum. Layout via HorizontalLayoutGroup or manual anchored positions. Update via RectTransform.sizeDelta or scale. No need for meshes or instancing at this scale.
decisionRules.evidence: Unity UGUI handles low hundreds of Graphics without measurable cost; the overhead is only visible past ~1000 elements on the same Canvas.

decisionRules.scenario: Visualization has 100–1,000 points in world space
decisionRules.rule: Use per-instance cube/quad GameObjects with a MaterialPropertyBlock for color. Allocate once in a setup phase; update scale or position via Transform or MaterialPropertyBlock per frame. No per-frame Instantiate.
decisionRules.evidence: MaterialPropertyBlock keeps SRP batching intact; Transform updates at this scale stay sub-millisecond on mid-range hardware.

decisionRules.scenario: Visualization has > 1,000 points or needs > 60 fps under streaming updates
decisionRules.rule: Switch to Graphics.RenderMeshIndirect (Unity 2022+) or DrawMeshInstancedIndirect with a StructuredBuffer of per-instance data. Update SetData per frame; geometry is never rebuilt. Follow kb-rendering-compute-buffer-lifecycle for OnEnable/OnDisable buffer lifecycle.
decisionRules.evidence: Single draw call + GPU-side read of per-instance data scales to 100k+ points on commodity GPUs.

decisionRules.scenario: Chart must update continuously from a data stream
decisionRules.rule: Maintain a ring buffer of fixed capacity. Overwrite oldest slot on new sample. Drive the visualization from the ring via buffer-in-place updates — no allocations in the hot path.
decisionRules.evidence: Standard streaming-chart pattern; avoids GC spikes visible at > 30 Hz update rates.

decisionRules.scenario: Visualization encodes a continuous scalar via color
decisionRules.rule: Use viridis or magma palette. Sample from a 256-texel LUT texture indexed by normalized value. Do NOT use HSV rainbow — it is perceptually non-uniform and colorblind-unfriendly.
decisionRules.evidence: Kovesi (2015) 'Good Colour Maps: How to Design Them' demonstrates the perceptual-uniformity failure of rainbow palettes.

decisionRules.scenario: Network graph has < 1k nodes and layout is wanted at runtime
decisionRules.rule: Run Fruchterman-Reingold for 100–300 iterations on CPU. Write final positions into the instance buffer. For > 1k nodes, move the integrator to a compute shader or bake layouts offline.
decisionRules.evidence: Fruchterman-Reingold (1991) is O(N^2) per iteration; 1k nodes * 300 iterations ≈ 3e8 ops, borderline on CPU main thread.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Frame-time scales linearly with number of data points.
commonPitfalls.cause: One GameObject per datum — Instantiate, Transform updates, and scene-graph overhead dominate past a few hundred.
commonPitfalls.fix: Replace the per-datum GameObject with instanced rendering. Store per-instance data in a StructuredBuffer, drive with Graphics.RenderMeshIndirect.

commonPitfalls.symptom: GC spike every time data refreshes.
commonPitfalls.cause: Rebuilding meshes (new Mesh()), re-creating materials, or setting new strings on TMP labels — all allocate managed memory.
commonPitfalls.fix: Allocate persistent Mesh/Material/StringBuilder once. Use Mesh.SetVertices + MarkDynamic, MaterialPropertyBlock for values, SetText(StringBuilder) for labels.

commonPitfalls.cause: The data list was re-sorted between frames; instance slot N now corresponds to a different row.
commonPitfalls.fix: Keep the slot assignment stable across frames. If you must sort for display, animate scale/color in place — do not permute slot indices.

commonPitfalls.cause: One quad per cell (10k GameObjects) or rebuilding a mesh per frame.
commonPitfalls.fix: Render as a single Texture2D (or compute-shader-driven RenderTexture) on one quad. SetPixels32 once per data change, not per frame unless the data changes per frame.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Rainbow palette makes value '5' and value '9' look closer than '1' and '5'.
commonPitfalls.cause: HSV hue cycle is not perceptually uniform — yellow is brighter than its neighbors and compresses adjacent values.
commonPitfalls.fix: Swap to viridis or magma. Encode as a 256-texel LUT and sample via (value - min) / (max - min).

commonPitfalls.symptom: Tooltips lag behind the mouse by a frame and flicker.
commonPitfalls.cause: Tooltip GameObject Instantiated/Destroyed on every hover event.
commonPitfalls.fix: Use a pool of 1–4 tooltip Canvas panels. Toggle .enabled and update text/position — never Instantiate per hover.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Network graph nodes spread infinitely or collapse to a point.
commonPitfalls.cause: Force-directed layout without damping, or spring constant / repulsion ratio not balanced.
commonPitfalls.fix: Add velocity damping (multiply velocity by ~0.85 per step) and clamp max displacement per iteration. Tune repulsion k so equilibrium edge length ≈ sqrt(area / N).

versionDeltas.change: Graphics.RenderMeshIndirect added alongside GraphicsBuffer as the preferred instanced-indirect path. Replaces DrawMeshInstancedIndirect for new code.
versionDeltas.migrationNote: Existing ComputeBuffer + DrawMeshInstancedIndirect code still works; new scripts on 2022+ should prefer the new API.

versionDeltas.change: Unity 6 documents RenderMeshIndirect + GraphicsBuffer as primary. UI Toolkit continues to mature alongside UGUI; for chart overlays UGUI + TextMeshPro remains the most stable path.

externalReferences.title: Unity Manual — UGUI Canvas and UI Components

sources.title: Fruchterman & Reingold (1991) — Graph Drawing by Force-directed Placement
Sugiyama, Tagawa, Toda (1981) — Methods for Visual Understanding of Hierarchical System Structures
Kovesi (2015) — Good Colour Maps: How to Design Them
van der Walt & Smith — matplotlib viridis colormap rationale
Okabe & Ito — Color Universal Design palette
Edward Tufte — The Visual Display of Quantitative Information (cited for data-ink ratio and small-multiples principles; not quoted)

authorshipNotes: Content authored by the Mosaic team from publicly-documented Unity Manual and ScriptReference pages (UGUI, MaterialPropertyBlock, Mesh, Texture2D, Graphics.RenderMeshIndirect, LineRenderer, Camera.ScreenPointToRay) together with open-literature references on graph layout (Fruchterman-Reingold 1991, Sugiyama 1981), perceptually-uniform colormaps (Kovesi 2015, van der Walt & Smith viridis rationale), and colorblind-safe palettes (Okabe-Ito). Edward Tufte's work is cited as a conceptual touchstone for data-ink and small-multiples reasoning but not quoted. API names, method signatures, and numeric layout constants are facts (not copyrightable); all prose, decision rules, llmGuidance, and pitfall analysis are original Mosaic editorial content. External references point to Unity documentation and cited papers; none of their text is reproduced here.
